critic_raw:
FamNet's few-shot counting methodology has specific evaluation design problems that the description makes visible.

The test-time adaptation strategy is the most novel and most problematic claim. The network adapts to a novel category at test time using exemplar objects from the test image itself. This creates a direct information leak from test data: the adaptation uses test image content to modify the network's parameters before prediction. If adaptation is allowed, FamNet's test-time performance is not comparable to baselines that do not adapt — the comparison is between a transductive method and inductive methods. If all competing methods also use test-time adaptation, this must be specified. If they do not, FamNet's improvement may entirely reflect the adaptation advantage rather than the architecture's quality.

The few-shot counting evaluation uses 3 exemplars per image. The paper claims that 'increasing the number of exemplars reduces error.' This is an expected property of any few-shot method — more supervision reduces uncertainty — and does not constitute evidence of a specific architectural advantage. The meaningful evaluation is: at k exemplars, does FamNet outperform baseline few-shot counters? And does the error reduction rate with increasing k match or exceed theoretical expectations? The description does not characterize this.

FSC-147 is a newly introduced dataset, and FamNet is trained and evaluated on it. When a method proposes its own benchmark dataset, there is a structural risk that the dataset's properties favor the proposed method's assumptions. Specifically, FamNet's assumption that exemplars are drawn from the same image as the query region may be more achievable for the visual categories chosen for FSC-147 than for categories not in the dataset. Independent evaluation on an existing few-shot counting benchmark is needed to decouple method quality from dataset design.

The evaluation against 'object detectors' as a comparison is a category error. Object detectors require per-class training with annotated bounding boxes — they are not few-shot and cannot generalize to novel categories without retraining. Comparing FamNet to an object detector tests a different capability (generalization to novel categories with exemplar supervision vs. within-category detection after full supervision) rather than the few-shot counting quality. The meaningful baseline is other few-shot counting methods, not detection methods.
all_issues_raised: Test-time adaptation uses test image content to modify network parameters before prediction, making FamNet transductive; comparing against inductive baselines that do not adapt conflates the architectural advantage of FamNet with the general advantage of transductive inference.

Demonstrating that more exemplars reduce error is an expected property of any few-shot method and does not evidence architectural quality; the meaningful comparison is per-k exemplar performance versus competitive few-shot counting baselines.

FamNet is trained and evaluated on FSC-147, a dataset it proposes — the dataset's visual categories may be selected in ways that favor FamNet's same-image exemplar assumption, and independent evaluation on an existing benchmark is needed to decouple method quality from dataset design.

Comparison against object detectors is a category error because detectors are not few-shot and cannot generalize to novel categories without retraining; they test a different capability and are not valid baselines for few-shot counting.
all_issues_adjudicated: Test-time adaptation uses test image content to modify network parameters before prediction, making FamNet transductive; comparing against inductive baselines that do not adapt conflates the architectural advantage of FamNet with the general advantage of transductive inference.

Demonstrating that more exemplars reduce error is an expected property of any few-shot method and does not evidence architectural quality; the meaningful comparison is per-k exemplar performance versus competitive few-shot counting baselines.

FamNet is trained and evaluated on FSC-147, a dataset it proposes — the dataset's visual categories may be selected in ways that favor FamNet's same-image exemplar assumption, and independent evaluation on an existing benchmark is needed to decouple method quality from dataset design.

Comparison against object detectors is a category error because detectors are not few-shot and cannot generalize to novel categories without retraining; they test a different capability and are not valid baselines for few-shot counting.
